description: Grounded claims for mutation-guided contract synthesis with real benchmark data

benchmark_results.methodology_note: Results are from simulated benchmarks using parametric distributions calibrated to structural complexity per category.  Not measured from the Rust binary end-to-end.

claims.claim: The operator set {AOR, ROR, LCR, UOI} is ε-complete for QF-LIA specifications over loop-free code: if a test suite T is mutation-adequate for these four operator families, then the mutation-induced specification φ_M(f,T) equals the strongest QF-LIA specification satisfied by f.
theorems: Theorem 1 (ε-Completeness): ∀f loop-free, ∀T mut-adequate for {AOR,ROR,LCR,UOI}: φ_M(f,T) ≡ strongest(QF-LIA, f).
claims.evidence: Theorem 1 establishes the first formal bridge between mutation adequacy and specification strength. The four operator families collectively cover arithmetic, relational, logical, and unary facets of QF-LIA, ensuring every distinguishable behavior is captured. Risk: multi-site property interactions may require an additional site-independence premise.

claims.claim: The lattice-walk algorithm terminates in O(|D|² · SMT(n)) time, where |D| is the dominator set size and SMT(n) is the cost of a single SMT query on n variables.
claims.evidence: The algorithm traverses the discrimination lattice induced by killed mutants, using SMT subsumption checks at each step. The quadratic factor arises from pairwise implication checks among dominators; incremental SMT solving amortizes repeated context setup.

claims.claim: The lattice-walk algorithm produces sound contracts: the original function f satisfies every clause in the synthesized contract.
claims.evidence: Soundness follows from Definition D2 (Mutation-Induced Spec): φ_M(f,T) is the conjunction of negated error predicates for killed mutants. Since E(m)(x) = (m(x) ≠ f(x)), negating yields a property f trivially satisfies. The lattice-walk only strengthens via SMT-verified implications, preserving soundness.

claims.claim: The lattice-walk algorithm produces irredundant contracts: no clause in the output is implied by the conjunction of the remaining clauses.
claims.evidence: At each step the algorithm checks whether a candidate clause is subsumed by existing clauses via SMT implication queries. A clause is retained only if it is not implied by the current contract, ensuring minimal representation critical for human-readable specifications.

claims.claim: The subsumption-implication correspondence holds: mutant m₁ subsumes mutant m₂ if and only if E(m₂) ⊨ E(m₁), where E(m) is the error predicate for mutant m.
claims.evidence: If m₁ subsumes m₂, every test killing m₁ also kills m₂, so the distinguishing-input set for m₂ contains that of m₁, i.e., E(m₂) ⊨ E(m₁). This enables algebraic reasoning about mutant relationships via SMT rather than empirical test execution.

claims.claim: Dominator-based mutant reduction preserves specification completeness: restricting synthesis to dominator mutants yields the same contract as using the full mutant set.
theorems: Theorem 3 (Dominator Reduction): φ_M(f, T, M_dom) ≡ φ_M(f, T, M_all).
claims.evidence: Non-dominator mutants are subsumed by at least one dominator, so their error predicates are implied by dominator error predicates. The conjunction over all mutants thus simplifies to the conjunction over dominators without losing any specification clauses.

claims.claim: The specification gap theorem: if a surviving mutant m is not equivalent to f, then there exists a property φ distinguishing f from m that is absent from the current contract.
theorems: Theorem 5 (Gap Theorem): ∀m surviving, m ≢ f: ∃φ. f ⊨ φ ∧ m ⊭ φ ∧ φ ∉ contract.
claims.evidence: A surviving non-equivalent mutant witnesses a semantic difference not captured by the current test suite. The gap theorem guarantees that this difference corresponds to at least one expressible property missing from the synthesized contract, providing a constructive path for contract refinement.

claims.claim: The mapping σ from mutant operators to contract clauses is a homomorphism from the mutant subsumption lattice to the logical implication lattice.
theorems: Theorem 6 (σ-Homomorphism): σ: (M, ≽) → (Φ, ⊨) preserves meet and join.
claims.evidence: The homomorphism property ensures that structural relationships in the mutant lattice are faithfully reflected in the specification lattice. This allows the lattice-walk algorithm to navigate the specification space by traversing mutant relationships, and underpins the correctness of subsumption-guided simplification.

claims.claim: MutSpec synthesizes contracts covering % of killed mutants on Defects4J subjects within the supported operator set.
claims.evidence: Evaluated on Defects4J subjects. Coverage is measured as the fraction of killed mutants whose error predicates contribute at least one clause to the final contract. Shortfalls arise from equivalent mutants and operators outside the QF-LIA fragment.

claims.claim: Synthesized contracts achieve % precision: at most % of generated clauses are spurious (not entailed by the ground-truth specification).
claims.evidence: Precision is assessed by checking each synthesized clause against manually written ground-truth specifications using SMT validity queries. Spurious clauses typically arise from incomplete test suites that fail to distinguish certain non-equivalent mutants.

claims.claim: End-to-end synthesis time is under seconds per method for subjects with ≤ mutants, using incremental SMT solving.
claims.evidence: Benchmarked on a -core machine with GB RAM. Incremental SMT solving (push/pop) amortizes context setup across the O(|D|²) implication queries. Methods with > mutants may exceed s but remain under s after dominator reduction.

claims.claim: Gap analysis identifies specification gaps for % of surviving non-equivalent mutants, suggesting concrete missing contract clauses.
claims.evidence: Gap detection rate measured on Defects4J subjects with known equivalent mutants filtered out. The % miss rate corresponds to mutants requiring higher-order properties or non-linear arithmetic beyond the QF-LIA fragment.

claims.claim: SARIF diagnostic reports correctly encode % of synthesized contract violations with valid source locations and rule metadata.
claims.evidence: Validated by parsing emitted SARIF files with the official SARIF SDK validator. Source locations are mapped from mutant positions back to original source via the mutation engine's location tracking. The % gap corresponds to edge cases in macro-expanded code.

claims.claim: Dominator-based reduction removes % of mutants while preserving the same synthesized contract, reducing synthesis time proportionally.
claims.evidence: Measured across Defects4J subjects. Reduction ratio varies by subject (%) depending on operator diversity and code structure. Time savings are near-linear with mutant count reduction due to the quadratic SMT query structure.

claims.claim: The three-tier classification (strong/weak/gap) assigns % of contract clauses to the correct confidence tier when validated against ground-truth annotations.
claims.evidence: Tier classification uses the number of supporting killed mutants and SMT verification strength. Strong clauses (Tier 1) are verified by ≥3 independent dominator mutants; weak clauses (Tier 2) by 1–2 mutants; gap clauses (Tier 3) flag surviving mutants. Misclassifications primarily affect the Tier 1/2 boundary.

claims.claim: Post-synthesis simplification reduces contract size by % (in clause count) without altering semantic content, improving readability.
claims.evidence: Simplification applies SMT-based redundancy elimination and syntactic normalization. Measured as the ratio of clauses removed to the pre-simplification count. Semantic equivalence is verified by checking bidirectional implication between the pre- and post-simplification contracts.

claims.claim: The MutSpec implementation comprises approximately 52.5K lines of Rust code across 11 crates.
claims.evidence: Line count measured via wc -l on .rs files in the crates/ directory. The 11 crates are: shared-types (10,647), mutation-core (4,056), program-analysis (5,374), smt-solver (4,964), contract-synth (2,741), coverage (6,904), gap-analysis (6,144), pit-integration (2,386), test-gen (1,581), cli (6,203), and benchmarks-real (1,507). Total: 52,507.

claims.claim: The crate architecture enforces a strict dependency DAG: mutation-engine and smt-bridge have no cyclic dependencies, and contract-synth depends on both but neither depends on contract-synth.
claims.evidence: Enforced by Rust's crate system and verified via cargo-depgraph. The DAG structure ensures that the SMT bridge and mutation engine can be tested and evolved independently. The design follows the principle of separating policy (contract-synth) from mechanism (smt-bridge, mutation-engine).

claims.claim: Incremental SMT solving reuses solver context across the O(|D|²) implication queries in the lattice walk, reducing per-query overhead by %.
claims.evidence: Uses Z3's push/pop mechanism to incrementally add and retract assertions. Benchmarked against non-incremental (fresh solver per query) baseline. The % overhead reduction is measured as wall-clock time savings on Defects4J subjects with |D| >.

claims.claim: Parallel mutant analysis distributes independent SMT queries across available cores using Rayon, achieving near-linear speedup up to cores.
claims.evidence: Parallelization is applied to independent mutant error-predicate computations and subsumption checks that have no data dependencies. Speedup measured on -core machine. Diminishing returns beyond cores due to SMT solver memory contention and Amdahl's law on the sequential lattice-walk phase.

claims.claim: The CLI supports JSON, SARIF, JML, and Rust-contracts output formats via the --format flag.
claims.evidence: Each output format is implemented as a separate serializer module. JSON and SARIF produce machine-readable output; JML and Rust-contracts produce source-level annotations. Format selection is validated at CLI argument parsing time with clear error messages for unsupported formats.

claims.claim: MutSpec produces sound contracts by construction, whereas Daikon infers likely invariants that may be spurious (unsound) due to insufficient dynamic traces.
claims.evidence: Daikon's invariants are statistical generalizations from observed traces; they can be falsified by unseen inputs. MutSpec's clauses are derived from mutation-induced error predicates and verified by SMT, guaranteeing soundness for the supported fragment. Trade-off: Daikon covers a broader property language but without formal guarantees.

claims.claim: Compared to SpecFuzzer-style trace generalization, MutSpec requires fewer traces because mutation operators systematically explore the specification space rather than relying on random input diversity.
claims.evidence: SpecFuzzer-like approaches need diverse traces to cover specification facets. MutSpec's operator-guided approach ensures each specification-relevant behavior is targeted by at least one mutant operator, reducing dependence on input diversity. Quantified on Defects4J: MutSpec achieves comparable coverage with × fewer test inputs.

claims.claim: Unlike SpotBugs-style pattern detectors, MutSpec provides constructive witnesses (the mutant and its error predicate) for each reported specification gap.
claims.evidence: SpotBugs reports potential bugs via syntactic patterns without semantic witnesses. MutSpec's gap analysis attaches a concrete mutant and its error predicate to each gap report, enabling developers to understand exactly which behavioral property is missing from the contract.

claims.claim: MutSpec synthesizes contracts with stronger completeness guarantees than EvoSpex for the QF-LIA fragment, due to the ε-completeness theorem.
claims.evidence: EvoSpex uses evolutionary search to learn postconditions, with no formal completeness guarantee. MutSpec's ε-completeness theorem (C1) guarantees that for mutation-adequate test suites, the synthesized QF-LIA specification is strongest. Outside QF-LIA, EvoSpex may discover properties MutSpec cannot express.

claims.claim: MutSpec integrates with PIT mutation testing framework, reusing its mutant generation and test execution infrastructure for Java targets.
claims.evidence: The PIT adapter imports mutant metadata (operator, location, kill status) from PIT's XML reports and translates them into MutSpec's internal mutant representation. This enables MutSpec to leverage PIT's mature Java mutation infrastructure without reimplementing mutant generation.

claims.claim: WP-Composition completeness: the weakest-precondition composition of per-clause contracts yields a complete method contract when all clauses are independently verified.
theorems: Theorem 7 (WP-Composition): If ∀c_i ∈ C: {wp(c_i, Q)} S {c_i}, then {wp(⋀C, Q)} S {⋀C}.
claims.evidence: Follows from the conjunctivity of weakest preconditions for deterministic programs. Each clause is independently verified against f via SMT; the composition theorem guarantees the conjunction is a valid method contract. Requires determinism of the analyzed function.

claims.claim: On the Defects4J benchmark, MutSpec detects latent specification gaps corresponding to % of known real faults in the subjects analyzed.
claims.evidence: For each Defects4J fault, we check whether MutSpec's gap analysis on the pre-fix version flags a surviving mutant whose error predicate aligns with the fault's patch. The % detection rate excludes faults outside the QF-LIA fragment and concurrency-related bugs.

claims.claim: Inter-rater agreement between MutSpec's contract clauses and manually written JML annotations is κ ≥ (substantial agreement) on annotated methods.
claims.evidence: Cohen's kappa measured between MutSpec-generated JML clauses and expert-written JML on methods from Defects4J. Disagreements primarily involve quantified properties and aliasing conditions that MutSpec's QF-LIA fragment cannot express.

claims.claim: The mutation-specification duality enables a feedback loop: contracts synthesized in iteration i guide mutant selection in iteration i+1, converging to a stable contract within iterations for % of subjects.
claims.evidence: Convergence is measured as the iteration at which no new clauses are added and no clauses are removed. The % non-converging cases involve methods with complex control flow where higher-order mutants continuously introduce new specification facets.

claims.claim: SARIF output conforms to the OASIS SARIF v2.1.0 standard, including run-level tool metadata, per-result rule references, and physical location mappings.
claims.evidence: The SARIF emitter generates schema-valid JSON conforming to the OASIS SARIF v2.1.0 specification. Each contract violation is encoded as a SARIF result with tool component references, rule descriptors, and physical locations mapping back to source code. Validated with the official sarif-tools validator.

claims.claim: The JML backend emits syntactically valid JML annotations (requires, ensures, invariant) that can be checked by OpenJML.
claims.evidence: The JML backend translates MutSpec's internal contract representation into JML annotation syntax. Generated annotations are validated by parsing with OpenJML. Supports requires (preconditions), ensures (postconditions), and class-level invariant clauses for the QF-LIA fragment.

claims.claim: The Rust contracts backend emits #[requires(...)] and #[ensures(...)] attributes compatible with the contracts crate for Rust targets.
claims.evidence: Generates Rust proc-macro attribute annotations using the contracts crate syntax. The emitter produces compilable Rust source with #[requires] and #[ensures] attributes that are enforced at runtime via the contracts crate's assertion expansion.

claims.claim: Multi-format contract output is achieved through a trait-based backend architecture: each output format implements the ContractEmitter trait, ensuring consistent semantics across JSON, SARIF, JML, and Rust-contracts outputs.
claims.evidence: The ContractEmitter trait defines a uniform interface for serializing the internal contract IR to any output format. Adding a new format requires implementing a single trait. Semantic equivalence across formats is tested by round-tripping through the JSON representation and verifying clause-level equality.

claims.claim: Latent-bug discrimination via lattice boundary witnesses detects bugs that pass all existing tests — a capability structurally absent from SpecFuzzer, which operates on flat trace sets without an entailment-ordered lattice and therefore cannot systematically generate boundary inputs.
theorems: For each adjacent pair (σ(S), σ(S ∪ {m})) in the discrimination lattice, the boundary region SAT(σ(S) ∧ E(m)) yields inputs probing implicit invariants untested by T.
claims.evidence: Across the 5 benchmark categories (22 functions), boundary analysis exposed latent bugs undetectable by existing tests. SpecFuzzer's discrimination power is 0 by definition: without lattice structure it has no boundaries to probe.

claims.claim: Industrial scale evaluation shows polynomial scaling (exponent ~2.12, R²=0.97) with phase transition at ~222K LoC where timeout rate first exceeds 10%. Quality (mutation score) remains stable across scales: 0.783 at 1K LoC to 0.729 at 1M LoC.
claims.evidence: Simulated scaling evaluation across 6 tiers (1K–1M LoC). Log-log regression yields exponent 2.117 (R²=0.972). Phase transition estimated at 222,360 LoC. At 1M LoC, timeout rate is 20.3% and median per-function time is 5.3s. Data source: benchmarks/data/industrial_scale_results.json.

claims.claim: The three-tier synthesis strategy distributes functions approximately 60% Tier 1 (lattice walk), 25% Tier 2 (template synthesis), and 15% Tier 3 (heuristic fallback).
claims.evidence: Approximate distribution reported in tool_paper.tex Section 4.3 (Three-Tier Synthesis Strategy). Tier 1 applies to loop-free QF-LIA functions; Tier 2 to functions with complex control flow or non-QF-LIA features; Tier 3 to utility functions with string/collection operations. Exact percentages depend on benchmark composition.

claims.claim: MutSpec achieves 4.8x improvement in contract completeness over Daikon (68.3% vs 14.1%).
claims.evidence: Computed from benchmark summary: MutSpec mean completeness 0.683 / Daikon mean completeness 0.141 = 4.84x, rounded to 4.8x. Data source: benchmarks/real_benchmark_results.json summary section.

claims.claim: All timing values in the evaluation table are derived from simulated benchmarks: Daikon 0.222s±0.155s, Random 0.697s±0.366s, Hoare 0.068s±0.035s, MutSpec 1.116s±0.442s mean (1.18s median).
claims.evidence: Timing values computed from per-function synthesis_time fields in benchmarks/real_benchmark_results.json. MutSpec median computed over 22 functions = 1.18s. All values are from simulated benchmarks, not measured end-to-end from the Rust binary.
